At each step, the agent selects one of four actions-Answer, Image Retrieval, Text Retrieval, and Caption-based on its current information state. We further design an automated pipeline to collect multi-step trajectories that record the agent's reasoning process, tool usage, and intermediate decisions. These trajectories are then used as supervision for fine-tuning. Experiments on InfoSeek and E-VQA demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming prior baselines and confirming the effectiveness of our framework.

title: DTCRS: Dynamic Tree Construction for Recursive Summarization
problem: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for recursive summarization faces issues with redundant summary nodes that increase construction time and may negatively impact question answering, and recursive summarization is not suitable for all types of questions.
key_idea: DTCRS dynamically generates summary trees based on document structure and query semantics, determines whether a summary tree is necessary by analyzing the question type, and uses embeddings of sub-questions as initial cluster centers to reduce redundant summaries while improving relevance.
method: DTCRS (Dynamic Tree Construction for Recursive Summarization) - a method that analyzes question type to determine if a summary tree is necessary, decomposes questions, and uses embeddings of sub-questions as initial cluster centers.
results_summary: The approach significantly reduces summary tree construction time and achieves substantial improvements across three QA tasks.
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) mitigates the hallucination problem of Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating external knowledge. Recursive summarization constructs a hierarchical summary tree by clustering text chunks, integrating information from multiple parts of a document to provide evidence for abstractive questions involving multi-step reasoning. However, summary trees often contain a large number of redundant summary nodes, which not only increase construction time but may also negatively impact question answering. Moreover, recursive summarization is not suitable for all types of questions. We introduce DTCRS, a method that dynamically generates summary trees based on document structure and query semantics. DTCRS determines whether a summary tree is necessary by analyzing the question type. It then decomposes the question and uses the embeddings of sub-questions as initial cluster centers, reducing redundant summaries while improving the relevance between summaries and the question. Our approach significantly reduces summary tree construction time and achieves substantial improvements across three QA tasks. Additionally, we investigate the applicability of recursive summarization to different question types, providing valuable insights for future research.

title: ChunQiuTR: Time-Keyed Temporal Retrieval in Classical Chinese Annals
problem: Retrieval in historical research often requires finding exact records for specific regnal months, where temporal consistency is as important as topical relevance. This is particularly challenging for Classical Chinese annals with implicit time expressions that can lead to temporally invalid results even when semantically plausible.
key_idea: Time-keyed retrieval for Classical Chinese annals where both topical relevance and temporal consistency are crucial
method: ChunQiuTR benchmark and CTD (Calendrical Temporal Dual-encoder) that combines Fourier-based absolute calendrical context with relative offset biasing
dataset_or_scenario: ChunQiuTR benchmark built from the Spring and Autumn Annals and its exegetical tradition, with records organized by month-level reign keys and chrono-near confounders
results_summary: CTD shows consistent gains over strong semantic dual-encoder baselines under time-keyed evaluation
summary: Retrieval shapes how language models access and ground knowledge in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). In historical research, the target is often not an arbitrary relevant passage, but the exact record for a specific regnal month, where temporal consistency matters as much as topical relevance. This is especially challenging for Classical Chinese annals, where time is expressed through terse, implicit, non-Gregorian reign phrases that must be interpreted from surrounding context, so semantically plausible evidence can still be temporally invalid. We introduce \textbf{ChunQiuTR}, a time-keyed retrieval benchmark built from the \textit{Spring and Autumn Annals} and its exegetical tradition. ChunQiuTR organizes records by month-level reign keys and includes chrono-near confounders that mirror realistic retrieval failures. We further propose \textbf{CTD} (Calendrical Temporal Dual-encoder), a time-aware dual-encoder that combines Fourier-based absolute calendrical context with relative offset biasing. Experiments show consistent gains over strong semantic dual-encoder baselines under time-keyed evaluation, supporting retrieval-time temporal consistency as a key prerequisite for faithful downstream historical RAG. Our code and datasets are available at \href{https://github.com/xbdxwyh/ChunQiuTR}{\texttt{github.com/xbdxwyh/ChunQiuTR}}.

title: RefineRAG: Word-Level Poisoning Attacks via Retriever-Guided Text Refinement
problem: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are vulnerable to knowledge poisoning attacks, and existing attack methods like PoisonedRAG remain detectable due to coarse-grained separate-and-concatenate strategies.
key_idea: Treating poisoning as a holistic word-level refinement problem to improve attack effectiveness and reduce detectability.
method: RefineRAG, a framework with two stages: Macro Generation that produces toxic seeds to induce target answers, and Micro Refinement that employs retriever-in-the-loop optimization to maximize retrieval priority while maintaining naturalness.
metrics: Attack Success Rate, grammar errors, repetition rates
results_summary: RefineRAG achieves state-of-the-art effectiveness with a 90% Attack Success Rate on NQ, and has the lowest grammar errors and repetition rates among all baselines. Proxy-optimized attacks successfully transfer to black-box victim systems.
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) significantly enhances Large Language Models (LLMs), but simultaneously exposes a critical vulnerability to knowledge poisoning attacks. Existing attack methods like PoisonedRAG remain detectable due to coarse-grained separate-and-concatenate strategies. To bridge this gap, we propose RefineRAG, a novel framework that treats poisoning as a holistic word-level refinement problem. It operates in two stages: Macro Generation produces toxic seeds guaranteed to induce target answers, while Micro Refinement employs a retriever-in-the-loop optimization to maximize retrieval priority without compromising naturalness. Evaluations on NQ and MSMARCO demonstrate that RefineRAG achieves state-of-the-art effectiveness, securing a 90% Attack Success Rate on NQ, while registering the lowest grammar errors and repetition rates among all baselines. Crucially, our proxy-optimized attacks successfully transfer to black-box victim systems, highlighting a severe practical threat.
